On Novig, a limit order lets you set the price you're willing to accept — it waits in the market until someone matches it at that price or better. A market order fills immediately at the best available price from counterparties already in the market.

Use a limit order when you're willing to wait for a specific price. Use a market order when you want to fill right away and don't mind taking the current best price.

A human on our team can walk you through examples if you want more detail on how this works on our platform specifically.

Novig Support

The charts spinning on load suggests a rendering issue on our end. A few quick things to try:

- Hard refresh your browser (Cmd+Shift+R on Mac, Ctrl+Shift+R on Windows).
- Clear your browser cache for novig.com and reload.
- Try the web app in an incognito/private window.

If that doesn't fix it, let us know which browser version you're on and whether the issue is still happening. We'll loop in engineering to investigate.

The order book matches orders on a price-time priority basis — best price first, then earliest timestamp. If two orders arrive at the same price in the same millisecond, the system has a tiebreaker, but the specifics of how that works I'd need a human to confirm for you.

On Novig, you're betting against other users, not against the house. When you place an order, it either matches against someone else's existing order on the other side, or it sits in the book waiting for a counterparty. If you want the exact mechanics of how simultaneous orders are handled, I can have someone follow up with details.

Novig Support
